design_philosophy.core_loop: Destroy all objects in a level through strategic ordering, avoiding enemies and environmental hazards. Player must mentally map dependencies between destructible elements to avoid trapping themselves.
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Resource scarcity (limited lives) vs. puzzle complexity. Each wrong move risks losing progress; puzzle demands forward planning without jump mobility.
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Precision demolition expert using a hammer to methodically clear construction sites while evading obstacles.
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Removes jump mechanic entirely—forces puzzle-first design philosophy over reflexive action. No optional difficulty settings; game enforces planning over execution. Competitive shift from original: '98 version emphasizes block-type destruction racing rather than exhaustive panel clearing.

difficulty.lives_system: Player starts with five lives; contact with enemies or fireballs costs one life. Game over when all lives depleted.
difficulty.checkpointing: Level select available from title screen—player can restart from any level, mitigating harsh death penalty.
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: High agency: player chooses starting level, can abort any level at will. Must abort if trapped. Strategic sequencing is entirely player-controlled.

progression.unlock_mechanism: 100 levels with free level select from start; no gating by completion.
replayability_hooks: Bonus scoring for chain destruction (bomb sequences)

world_building_techniques: Character archetypes (Mario as hero, Spike/Blackie as antagonist foreman)
Implicit narrative: enemy behavior (Spike knocks down objects to disrupt)

danger_signals: Destructible terrain that can trap player if sequenced incorrectly
Indestructible barrels and ladders that create environmental constraints
environmental_storytelling: Destructible vs. indestructible objects establish spatial rules
Enemy movement patterns suggest sabotage (Spike deliberately knocks down structures)
Doors mechanically redirect enemies into background (safety mechanic)

sources_used: wikipedia: Wrecking Crew (video game) — covers both original 1984 arcade/NES version and Wrecking Crew '98 SNES sequel
